case_name: Remote surface-water drinking supply with cis-1,2-dichloroethylene under pilot-only and capital constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for treating cis-1,2-dichloroethylene-impacted surface water for drinking use while balancing pilot-only deployment limits, low capital availability, and remote-site operability.

input_data.scenario_background: A remote utility serving a small island community draws from a reservoir that has episodic solvent contamination traced to runoff from an old industrial storage area near a tributary inlet. The utility must install a treatment upgrade quickly, but funding currently supports only pilot-scale modular systems that can later be expanded if performance is proven. The existing plant already uses basic clarification and disinfection, and the decision hinges on which add-on technology best balances reliable cis-1,2-dichloroethylene control against low upfront cost, simple operation, and manageable residuals in a location with infrequent specialist support.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve stable compliance with drinking-water requirements for cis-1,2-dichloroethylene and associated volatile chlorinated solvents under variable surface-water conditions.
secondary_goals: use modular equipment suitable for pilot-first deployment
keep operation practical for remote operators
avoid difficult residuals or off-site waste burdens where possible

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer minimal hazardous residual generation and avoid options requiring frequent specialized waste handling shipments
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: system must tolerate seasonal turbidity and organic matter swings and remain practical with limited on-site technical support

core_unit_functions.existing_clarification_and_filtration: control particulates and reduce fouling or scaling impacts on downstream stripping equipment
core_unit_functions.packed_tower_aeration_or_low_profile_air: transfer volatile cis-1,2-dichloroethylene and co-occurring chlorinated solvents from water to air
core_unit_functions.off_gas_vapor_phase_granular_activated_c: capture stripped volatile organics from air emissions for environmental compliance

applicability_rationale: cis-1,2-dichloroethylene is a volatile organic compound, making air stripping a well-established and effective removal route
for a remote site with only pilot-scale deployment available, modular aeration systems can be demonstrated and later expanded more readily than many liquid-phase treatment systems
this route avoids creating a contaminated liquid concentrate stream, which is advantageous where waste handling logistics are difficult
although off-gas treatment is required, overall process chemistry and operator tasks are still manageable compared with more advanced destructive or membrane systems
the existing surface-water clarification train can remain in service, reducing incremental capital needs
potential_risks: air stripping performance depends on maintaining proper air-to-water ratio and tower operation
off-gas carbon replacement must be planned to prevent atmospheric release of VOCs
cold weather or seasonal hydraulic variation can affect stripping efficiency and pilot results
surface-water solids carryover can impair equipment if pretreatment performance deteriorates

core_unit_functions.existing_clarification_and_filtration: reduce suspended solids loading and protect adsorber hydraulics
core_unit_functions.liquid_phase_granular_activated_carbon: adsorb cis-1,2-dichloroethylene and other trace organic contaminants from water

applicability_rationale: GAC is a widely used drinking-water technology and can address volatile organics without creating air emissions
containerized or skid-mounted GAC units are practical for pilot-scale demonstration in remote locations
operator familiarity is often higher for adsorption systems than for more complex oxidation or membrane systems
this route can also provide some co-benefit for background natural organic matter and taste-and-odor compounds
it ranks below air stripping because cis-1,2-dichloroethylene is often more economically handled by volatilization, while surface-water DOC can shorten carbon life and increase replacement frequency
potential_risks: natural organic matter competition can reduce adsorption capacity and increase media changeout frequency
spent carbon management and transport from a remote site require logistics planning
breakthrough monitoring must be conservative because influent concentrations may spike episodically
co-occurring VOCs may consume adsorption capacity faster than expected during pilot testing

core_unit_functions.existing_clarification_and_filtration: stabilize solids loading before pilot treatment
core_unit_functions.spray_aeration_or_diffused_aeration: provide partial volatilization of cis-1,2-dichloroethylene with simpler equipment than a packed tower
core_unit_functions.liquid_phase_granular_activated_carbon_p: capture residual dissolved VOCs after partial stripping

applicability_rationale: this hybrid route can be attractive when capital is very limited because it uses simpler aeration hardware combined with a smaller polishing adsorber
it offers a practical pilot-first strategy where the utility wants to test whether partial volatilization can reduce carbon consumption
the configuration spreads removal duty across two relatively understandable unit processes
it ranks below the top two options because spray or diffused aeration is generally less efficient and less predictable than dedicated packed-tower stripping for chlorinated VOCs
potential_risks: simple aeration may not consistently achieve sufficient removal during high influent events
without well-controlled off-gas management, air emissions concerns may remain
operational tuning between aeration and GAC polishing can be more involved than a single-process solution
surface-water temperature and flow variation may cause unstable performance

core_unit_functions.existing_clarification_and_filtration: lower particulate and UV-interfering load before oxidation
core_unit_functions.uv_hydrogen_peroxide_advanced_oxidation: oxidize cis-1,2-dichloroethylene and related chlorinated organics
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_biological_or_: remove oxidation byproducts and provide polishing

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation is technically capable of degrading chlorinated solvents rather than transferring them to another phase
the route can be appealing when future source-water contamination may broaden and destructive treatment is valued
however, it is downgraded because surface-water TOC and UV absorbance increase oxidant and energy demand, which is unfavorable for a capital-limited remote pilot site
chemical supply, process monitoring, and lamp system maintenance create a higher technical burden than adsorption or stripping
potential_risks: background organic matter can scavenge radicals and reduce treatment efficiency
incomplete oxidation may form intermediate products requiring careful verification
chemical handling and UV system maintenance may exceed local operator comfort
pilot optimization can be time-intensive under variable surface-water quality

core_unit_functions.enhanced_pretreatment: protect membranes from fouling by turbidity and organic matter
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: physically separate dissolved contaminants including cis-1,2-dichloroethylene and co-occurring compounds
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry for distribution compatibility

applicability_rationale: membrane treatment is technically capable and can provide broad-spectrum contaminant control
it may be considered if the utility expects future salinity or multi-contaminant challenges beyond VOCs alone
despite technical capability, it ranks last because it has the weakest fit to pilot-only, low-capital, and remote-operation constraints
the need for robust pretreatment, membrane maintenance, and concentrate management makes it poorly aligned with this case compared with simpler alternatives
potential_risks: concentrate disposal is difficult and expensive in a remote location
surface-water fouling potential drives pretreatment complexity and operational risk
membrane systems require more specialized oversight and spare parts support
pilot results may not translate economically to full implementation under current funding limits

preferred_rank_pattern: air_stripping_with_off_gas_control should rank near the top for volatile chlorinated solvent removal
GAC should appear as a credible alternative because of modularity and operational familiarity
advanced_oxidation and membrane options should rank lower due to pilot-scale, capital, and remote-site misalignment

must_reflect: pilot_only deployment favors modular and rapidly deployable treatment trains
remote_location penalizes options requiring high specialist support or difficult waste logistics
capital_constraint should reduce ranking of membrane-heavy or high-instrumentation systems

must_contain: why volatilization-based treatment is relevant for cis-1,2-dichloroethylene
why surface-water organic matter affects GAC and advanced oxidation performance
why technically capable options can rank lower when residuals, complexity, or capital fit are poor

should_mention: carbon breakthrough or media replacement logistics
surface-water variability effects on pilot performance
